steps.title: Set up coordinates for perpendicular chords
steps.content:
Place D at the origin, with AC along the y-axis and BE along the x-axis. Let AD = $a$ and CD = $c$. Then:
  • A = (0, $a$)
  • C = (0, $-c$)
  • B = ($-\sqrt{226}$, 0)
  • E = ($e$, 0) where $e$ = DE
All four points lie on the circle with diameter 115.

steps.title: Apply Power of a Point for intersecting chords
steps.content:
When two chords intersect at D inside a circle, the products of their segments are equal. Substituting BD = $\sqrt{226}$:
$$ac = \sqrt{226} \cdot e$$
Solving for $e$:
$$e = \frac{ac}{\sqrt{226}}$$

steps.title: Use the perpendicular chords theorem
steps.content:
For two perpendicular chords in a circle with diameter 115, the sum of squares of all four segments equals $115^2$:
$$a^2 + (\sqrt{226})^2 + c^2 + e^2 = 13225$$
Since $(\sqrt{226})^2 = 226$:
$$a^2 + c^2 + e^2 = 12999$$

steps.title: Solve for AD and CD
steps.content:
Substitute $e = \frac{ac}{\sqrt{226}}$ into the perpendicular chords constraint:
$$a^2 + c^2 + \frac{a^2c^2}{226} = 12999$$
Test $a = 2$:
$$4 + c^2 + \frac{4c^2}{226} = 12999$$
$$c^2\left(1 + \frac{4}{226}\right) = 12995$$
$$c^2 \cdot \frac{230}{226} = 12995$$
$$c^2 = 12769$$
$$c = 113$$
Therefore, $r = \frac{\text{CD}}{\text{AD}} = \frac{113}{2} = 56.5$.

Equivalent forms accepted: 113/2, 56.5.
